blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: that thread round his arm, that one will most assuredly obtain all worldly pleasures and obtain moksha, salvation.'

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Annapurna Worship—Annapurna literally means the “Supplier of Food.” It is one of the manifestations of Shiva's wife. She is seated on a throne, and holds a ladle, like the Græcian horn of plenty.

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Among the Chāndraseni Kāyasth Prabhus she is worshipped by the bride when the bridegroom is expected to arrive. She sits pouring coloured yellow rice on the image and repeating, 'Oh Goddess! give me home by protecting the honoured guest who has just arrived' (meaning the bridegroom).

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Āshādhi-Ekādashi—The eleventh day of the month of Āshādh (June-July). It is considered specially sacred. (See Ekādashi.)

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Ashvath (Ficus religiosa tree).—See Somavati for its folklore.

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Avali-Bhojan.—See under Vaikunth-Chaturdashi. It is a dinner taken under the Phylanthus emblica tree on the fourteenth day of Kārtik (October-November).

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Bāras—The twelfth day of a lunar month. For folklore, see Vasu-bāras, and Birth-day of Vāman (Vāman Dvādashi).

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Bhadra-Kāli—In the third chapter of Shivalilāmrit, a section of the Brahmottarkhand of the Skandhapurān, the origin of the Bhadra-Kāli of Gokarna-Mahābaleshwar in the Bombay Presidency has been described. This
